sections.paragraph:
Mem, the thirteenth Hebrew letter; as a numeral it stands for forty. The name of this letter מֵים probably signifies water, i.q. מַיִם, and its most ancient forms bore a resemblance to waves. In Ethiopic it is called Mai, i.e. water. [“To this answers the Greek name Μῦ, i.e. Phœnic. מו water.”]

It is interchanged-(a) often with the other labials, as ב and ף, which see [“and even with ו, e.g. תְּמָה and תְּוָה”]-(b) with liquids, especially Nun, comp. אִם; Syr. ܐܶܢ; Arab. إِنْ, أَنْ; בֹּהֶן; Arab. إِْبهَامُ thumb; בָּטְנִים pistacio; compare ܒܛܶܡܐܳ pistacia terebinthus, Lin., דָּשֵׁן; Arab. دسم to be fat, מֹורַג; ذَوْرَجُ a threshing wain; שָׂטם and שָׂטַן to be opposed to; ־ִים and ־ִין the mark of the plural; comp. the roots מָגַד, מָגַר, מֹוט, הוּד. More rarely with לְ see p. 421, A . As to Mem, dropped at the end of words, see Lgb. p. 136, 138; also as to the languages of the Hetrusci and Umbri, see O. Müllee, Hetrusker, t. i. p. 56.
